title: The practice of being kind to yourself on the days when nothing works
author: Kalesh

htmlContent:
The day when nothing works

I woke up this morning and everything felt wrong. My body was heavy. My mind was foggy. The simplest tasks felt like climbing a mountain in flip-flops. And the voice inside my head was not kind. It said things like "What's wrong with you?" and "You should be able to handle this by now" and "Everyone else has their life together, why don't you?"

You know that voice. We all do. It's the one that shows up on the days when you're already down and decides to kick you while you're there. Here's the thing. That voice is not your enemy. It's actually a survival mechanism that got installed a long time ago, probably when you needed harshness to survive. But right now, in this moment, it's not helping. It's making everything worse.

I spent years trying to fight that voice. I thought if I could just be disciplined enough, strong enough, perfect enough, it would shut up. I tried pushing harder. I tried ignoring my body's signals. I tried the "just do it" approach. And you know what happened? I crashed. Hard. My nervous system went into complete shutdown mode, and I couldn't get out of bed for three days.

Peter Levine, the founder of Somatic Experiencing, talks about how trauma and stress get trapped in the body. He says that our nervous system doesn't respond to logic or force. It responds to safety and connection. When you're having a day where nothing works, your nervous system is likely in a state of overwhelm or shutdown. And the last thing it needs is more pressure.

So what do we do on those days? How do we be kind to ourselves when everything in us is screaming that we don't deserve kindness? That's what I want to explore with you today. Because honestly, this is the hardest practice. It's easy to be kind to yourself when life is going well. It's a whole different thing when you're in the trenches.

Why self-kindness feels impossible on hard days

There's a reason why being kind to yourself feels so wrong on bad days. It's not because you're broken or defective. It's because your brain has learned a specific pattern. When things go wrong, your brain goes into threat-detection mode. It scans for what's wrong, what's dangerous, what needs to be fixed. And in that mode, kindness feels like letting your guard down.

Think about it. If you grew up in an environment where mistakes were punished, where vulnerability was exploited, where showing weakness was dangerous, then your brain learned that self-criticism is protection. Being harsh with yourself was a way to prevent future harm. It was a survival strategy. And survival strategies don't just disappear because you logically know they're not helpful anymore.

Bessel van der Kolk, author of "The Body Keeps the Score," has spent decades researching how trauma reshapes the brain and body. He says that the body keeps the score even when the mind wants to move on. So when you're having a day where nothing works, your body is probably holding onto old patterns of shame, fear, and self-rejection. And those patterns are strong. They've been reinforced thousands of times.

So the first thing I want to say is this. If you can't be kind to yourself right now, that's okay. It doesn't mean something is wrong with you. It means you're human, and you have a history. A practical guide to working with your nervous system is Anchored (paid link). It offers concrete practices for calming the nervous system without forcing yourself to feel things you don't feel.

I want you to notice what just happened in your body when you read that. Did your shoulders drop even slightly? Did your breath change? That's your nervous system responding to permission. Permission to not have it all figured out. Permission to struggle.

The smallest possible kindness

Here's what I've learned from years of trial and error, from reading the research, and from sitting with my own pain. Self-kindness on hard days cannot be big. It cannot be grand. It cannot be the kind of thing you see on Instagram with someone in a bathtub surrounded by candles and rose petals. That's not real life. That's a product being sold to you.

Real self-kindness on a day when nothing works looks like this. You drink a glass of water even though you don't want to. You put on socks that feel comfortable. You let yourself sit on the floor instead of forcing yourself to do the dishes. You say "I'm having a hard time" out loud to someone who won't try to fix you.

Wild, right? The most powerful practices are often the smallest ones. But that's because your nervous system responds to small signals of safety, not to grand gestures. A gentle touch on your own arm. A slow exhale. A moment of not pushing. These tiny acts of kindness send a message to your brain: "We're okay. We don't need to fight right now."

Janina Fisher, a leading expert on trauma treatment, talks about how we can learn to befriend our survival parts rather than fighting them. She says that every part of you that shows up, even the critical voice, even the shutdown, even the panic, is trying to help you survive. When you can see those parts with compassion instead of judgment, something shifts.

I tried this the other day when I was in full spiral mode. I put my hand on my chest and said to the scared part of me, "I see you. I know you're trying to protect me. Thank you for working so hard." It felt ridiculous at first. But then I noticed my breathing slow down. My jaw unclenched. The crisis didn't disappear, but it became manageable.

A gentle approach to working with shame and self-rejection is Radical Acceptance (paid link). Tara Brach's work is rooted in both Buddhist psychology and modern neuroscience, and it offers a way to hold yourself with kindness even when you feel like you don't deserve it.

What to do when you can't do anything

There are days when even the smallest act of self-kindness feels impossible. When your body is so heavy that moving feels like swimming through concrete. When your mind is so foggy that you can't remember what helps. When the voice inside is so loud that you can't hear anything else.

On those days, I want you to try something different. Don't try to be kind to yourself. That's too big. Instead, try to be neutral. Neutral is easier than kind. Neutral doesn't require warmth or love or acceptance. It just requires a pause. A moment of not making things worse.

Here's what neutral looks like. You're lying in bed and the critical voice is saying "You're lazy, get up." Instead of fighting it or agreeing with it, you just notice it. You say to yourself, "There's that thought again. Interesting." You don't have to believe it. You don't have to argue with it. You just observe it like a cloud passing through the sky.

Deb Dana, a clinician and author who specializes in Polyvagal Theory, talks about "glimmers." These are small moments of safety and connection that shift your nervous system out of survival mode. A glimmer can be the warmth of sunlight on your face. The sound of a bird outside your window. The feeling of a soft blanket against your skin. You don't have to create glimmers. You just have to notice them.

So on the days when nothing works, try this. Lie down somewhere comfortable. Put your hand on your belly. And just notice one thing that isn't terrible. Just one. It could be the way your pillow supports your head. The cool air on your skin. The fact that you're still breathing. Stay with that one thing for a few breaths. That's it. That's the practice.

I know it sounds too simple. But simplicity is the point. Your nervous system doesn't need complex solutions. It needs small, consistent signals of safety. It needs to know that you're not going to abandon yourself when things get hard.

A book that many find validates their childhood experience is Adult Children of Emotionally Immature Parents (paid link). Understanding where your patterns came from can make it easier to hold yourself with compassion rather than judgment.

The long, slow return to yourself

Here's what nobody tells you about healing. It's not a straight line. It's not a journey from broken to fixed. It's a spiral. You'll come back to the same places over and over again, but each time you'll be a little different. Each time you'll have a little more capacity. Each time you'll be able to hold yourself with a little more kindness.

I've been doing this work for over a decade. And I still have days when nothing works. I still have moments when the old voices take over and I feel like I'm back at square one. But here's what's different. I don't stay there as long. I know that the darkness will pass. I know that I can reach for help. And most , I know that being harsh with myself only makes things worse.

So if you're reading this on a day when nothing works, I want you to know something. You are not failing. You are not broken. You are having a human experience. And that experience includes hard days. It includes days when you can't do the things you want to do. Days when your body says no. Days when the best you can do is survive.

And that is enough. Surviving is enough. Being alive is enough. Showing up imperfectly is enough. You don't have to earn your own kindness. You don't have to prove that you deserve it. It's yours by right of being human.

Sit with that for a moment. Let it land in your body. You don't have to believe it yet. Just let it sit there. Let it be a possibility.

A practical guide to boundaries that many find accessible is Set Boundaries, Find Peace (paid link). Because sometimes being kind to yourself means saying no to things that drain you, even when it feels uncomfortable.

The practice of being kind to yourself on the days when nothing works is not about feeling good. It's about not making things worse. It's about interrupting the cycle of self-abandonment. It's about proving to your nervous system, one small moment at a time, that you are safe now. That you don't need to be perfect. That you can be held in your own arms, even when everything is falling apart.

I'm still learning this. I'll probably be learning it for the rest of my life. But I can tell you this much. It's worth it. The practice of returning to yourself, again and again, with kindness instead of cruelty, changes everything. It changes your brain. It changes your body. It changes the way you move through the world.

And on the days when nothing works, that change is still happening. Even when you can't feel it. Even when you don't believe it. The seeds are still growing underground. Spring will come again. It always does.

Healing Journey Resources

  • Anchored (paid link) - A practical guide to working with your nervous system
  • Set Boundaries, Find Peace (paid link) - A practical guide to boundaries that many find accessible

  • Radical Acceptance (paid link) - A gentle approach to working with shame and self-rejection
